title: Requirements for Witnessing
speaker: Dr. John Battle
source_files: Requirements for Witnessing - 1 Thessalonians 2_1-12 - Dr. John Battle.txt

outline.title: I. Introduction: Paul's Witness at Thessalonica
subpoints: Paul was in Thessalonica only a few weeks before being driven out
He left behind a church of converted Jews and Gentiles facing persecution
Critics accused Paul of self-interest — he uses chapter 2 to defend his ministry
Paul invites them to remember how he behaved — a pattern for us today

outline.title: II. Paul Was Bold in His Witness
subpoints: Despite suffering at Philippi, Paul was bold in God to speak the gospel
His boldness came from God, not from self-confidence
He spoke in the midst of much conflict and opposition

outline.title: III. Paul Was Sincere in His Witness (Verses 3-4)
subpoints: His exhortation did not come from error, uncleanness, or deceit
He was not pleasing men but God who tests hearts
Sincerity means speaking the word as God intends, not as men want to hear it

outline.title: IV. Paul Was Humble in His Witness (Verses 5-8)
subpoints: He did not use flattering words or seek glory from men
He could have made demands as an apostle of Christ but did not
Like a nursing mother cherishing her own children, Paul was gentle and serving
He imparted not only the gospel but his own life because they were dear to him

outline.title: V. Paul Was Generous in His Witness (Verse 9)
subpoints: He labored night and day as a tentmaker so as not to be a burden
He had a right to financial support but chose not to demand it
His generosity modeled that the gospel is given freely, not for personal profit

outline.title: VI. Paul Was Concerned for People in His Witness (Verses 10-12)
subpoints: God is his highest witness — he behaved devoutly, justly, and blamelessly
Like a father with his own children, he exhorted, comforted, and charged each one
He was stirred in his spirit by people's lost condition, as when he saw Athens full of idols
When we witness, our concern should be for the person, not just the argument

summary:
Dr. John Battle continues a series on being a good witness for Christ, drawing from Paul's account of his ministry in Thessalonica in 1 Thessalonians 2:1-12. Paul was defending his reputation against critics who said he ran away when the persecution got hot and left the congregation to bear the brunt. Paul's defense is an appeal to memory: you remember what I was like. Dr. Battle draws out three markers Paul uses to recall his time with the Thessalonians — introduced each time with the phrase 'you remember' — and identifies five qualities that characterized Paul's gospel witness: boldness, sincerity, humility, generosity, and genuine concern.

The sermon gives extended attention to Paul's humility, illustrated by a contrast with a contemporary political speaker who required luxury accommodations and a $225,000 fee. Paul, though he had apostolic authority to make demands, chose instead to be gentle like a nursing mother with her own child — serving rather than being served. His generosity is highlighted through his tent-making labor in Thessalonica, supplemented only by gifts from the Philippian church. The sermon closes with Paul's pastoral concern for people — like a father for his own children — modeled on Paul's being 'stirred in his spirit' when he saw the idols of Athens. Dr. Battle challenges listeners to look past arguments and debates to care for the actual people who need the gospel.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 1 Thessalonians 2:1-12

If you're able, please remain standing and turn in your Bibles to 1 Thessalonians 2. We will be reading verses 1 through 12.

For you yourselves know, brethren, that our coming to you was not in vain. But even after we had suffered before and were spitefully treated at Philippi, as you know, we were bold in our God to speak to you the gospel of God in much conflict. For our exhortation did not come from error or uncleanness, nor was it in deceit. But as we have been approved by God to be entrusted with the gospel, even so we speak, not as pleasing men, but God who tests our hearts. For neither at any time did we use flattering words, as you know, nor a cloak for covetousness — God is witness. Nor did we seek glory from men, either from you or from others, when we might have made demands as apostles of Christ. But we were gentle among you, just as a nursing mother cherishes her own children. So, affectionately longing for you, we were well pleased to impart to you not only the gospel of God, but also our own lives, because you had become dear to us. For you remember, brethren, our labor and toil; for laboring night and day, that we might not be a burden to any of you, we preached to you the gospel of God. You are witnesses, and God also, how devoutly and justly and blamelessly we behaved ourselves among you who believe. As you know how we exhorted and comforted and charged every one of you, as a father does his own children, that you would walk worthy of God who calls you into His kingdom and glory.

God bless the reading of His word. You may be seated.

## Introduction

It is a great privilege for me to introduce Dr. Battle to preach this morning. Thank you, Brother Scott. It is a pleasure to be here and fellowship with you once again in the Word of God.

Today we're continuing our thoughts on how to be a good witness for the Lord Jesus Christ. In Paul's own life, he went to the city of Thessalonica, and God gave him a very successful time there — though he was only there a few weeks, and when he left (chased out of town, basically), there was a church that had been established, made up of converted Jews and Gentiles. They met in homes, had just the beginnings of a congregation, but they were wanting to serve Christ and be loyal to Christ even though the authorities and the people around them were very hostile.

But there were some people in Thessalonica who were now criticizing Paul because Paul had run away and left them holding the bag. They said, well, he just came here for a short time, when it got hot he left, and now he's leaving us here to bear the brunt of this persecution. Maybe he's in this for his own benefit. So in chapter 2, Paul is responding to that criticism. He's saying, no — you know how we behaved among you.

In this passage, he recalls three different topics he introduces with the phrase "you remember." And so I like to compare what Paul did there with what we're supposed to be doing today.

## Paul Was Bold (Verse 2)

We saw in the first few verses of chapter 2 that as Paul shared the gospel, he was bold. Verse 2: But even after we had suffered before and were spitefully treated at Philippi, we were bold in our God to speak to you the gospel of God in much conflict.

His boldness came not from himself but from God. And it came in the face of real opposition — suffering at Philippi and conflict in Thessalonica.

## Paul Was Sincere (Verses 3-5)

Another aspect of his witness was his sincerity. Look at verse 3: For our exhortation did not come from error or uncleanness, nor was it in deceit. It was sincere. We didn't do it to get money from you. In verse 5: not a cloak for covetousness. But in verse 4 he says, we were not pleasing men but God who is testing our hearts.

So we've seen his boldness and his sincerity as he presented to them the gospel of God.

## Paul Was Humble (Verses 5-8)

Now the next section is what I'd like for us to especially look at this morning — a short list of things that characterized his ministry that we should be doing today. Paul says "you remember" three times to introduce these topics.

The first topic: Paul was humble among them — verses 5 through 8.

Verse 6: Nor did we seek glory from men, either from you or from others, when we might have made demands as apostles of Christ. He might have been a "weighty person." I looked up — back a few years to the 2016 election — a letter that was sent out about requirements for a visiting speaker. The fee was $225,000. She and her staff had to be put up in the presidential suite plus extra rooms, flown in on a private jet — a 737 at minimum. Then all phone bills, internet, food, drinks — all covered. I was thinking, you'd have to be a pretty deep-pocket charity to pay for this speaker. And I wonder if that speaking engagement would result in more income to the charity.

But that's the opposite of what Paul did. He says, we could have been weighty, we could have been burdensome, because we were apostles of Christ. We could have demanded that you treat us with all luxury. But we didn't do that. We didn't seek glory from men or anything else.

Rather, verse 7: we were gentle among you just as a nursing mother cherishes her own children. Most mothers don't demand things from their little babies — they give. And it uses the expression "her own children." She stays up late when they're sick. She takes care of them. She wakes up in the middle of the night when they're hungry. This is how Paul says we were with you. We were serving you in a humble way.

And in verse 8: So, affectionately longing for you, we were well pleased to impart to you not only the gospel of God, but also our own lives, because you had become dear to us.

So when people come to us or we talk to people about the Lord, it should be in an attitude of humility and service — not of superiority.

## Paul Was Generous (Verse 9)

Also notice his generosity — verse 9: For you remember, brethren, our labor and toil; for laboring night and day, that we might not be a burden to any of you, we preached to you the gospel of God.

Paul had a right to an income while he lived among them. He had the right to receive money for his support. But he didn't ask for it. Rather, he labored day and night — and the word in the original is plural, so along with him were his companions Silas and Timothy.

We know from other places that he was a tent-maker — or more precisely, a leather worker who sewed things together with his hands. In Acts 20, Paul preached to the elders and said, holding his hands up: these hands have labored. He labored at night — this candle didn't go out at night. And it was hard, unpleasant work — toil and labor, like a little play on words in the Greek.

But Paul did it so that he would not be indebted to them financially, so that he could preach the gospel freely. He was grateful for support when it came — he tells the Philippian church in Philippians 4:16 that even in Thessalonica they sent aid once and again for his necessities. So he was grateful for that support. But he did not insist that the church pay him a wage. And that's good for the Lord's testimony.

As we give out the gospel, our first thought isn't for what we get out of it. It's what we can give — the message of God — and our desire to benefit the person, not ourselves.

## Paul Was Concerned for People (Verses 10-12)

Finally, Paul was concerned — verse 10: You are witnesses, and God also — the highest witness, almost like an oath — how devoutly and justly and blamelessly we behaved ourselves among you who believe. As you know how we exhorted and comforted and charged every one of you as a father does his own children, that you would walk worthy of God who calls you into His own kingdom and glory.

Here he uses a very similar expression to before. Before he said, as a nursing mother with her own children. Now he says, as a father with his own children — his own children. It shows the great love that a father has for his children. And as a father brings up his children, he's concerned that they would be provided for, brought up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord, prepared for all the trials and tribulations of life. That's the concern Paul had for these people.

Sometimes you can tell if people care about other people or not. Paul said elsewhere that he was a debtor to the Greeks and to the Jews — he had an obligation to share with them the gospel. And when we see someone who doesn't know Christ, we have an obligation to tell them.

Remember what it said in Acts when Paul got to Athens and saw the whole city given over to idolatry — it says his spirit was stirred within him. And sometimes I get stirred up because people use bad arguments and I know the answer and I get all stirred up about that. But that's more of the argument itself — I don't really care about the people the argument is about. That's a theoretical level.

But Paul said when he saw those idols, his spirit was disturbed within him because all these people were worshiping idols and were lost without Christ, without hope in the world. And what was his response? He talked to these people. Not to go around smashing idols, but to talk to the people who were worshiping them. That's where the problem is. Look at the person saying these things. That's a heart that needs God.

## Conclusion

So as we witness to people, I trust that Paul's example there in Thessalonica will be an encouragement to us. We will see people in need of the gospel. We'll be concerned about them. We'll be generous with our time and our efforts as we talk to people and witness for Christ, and support with our money those who are going forth with the gospel. We'll pray for missionaries and the outreach of the gospel. And we will be humble, not expecting people to give us all kinds of glory and money, but doing it for love for Christ.

May God give us a faithful and good witness for Him.

Let us pray. Our Father, we give thanks for the privilege to bear witness to Christ. We thank You that the apostle gave us such a wonderful example here in his ministry in Thessalonica. Lord, we pray as we bear witness for Christ today — as You have called us by Your gospel, by Your truth to follow You and to bear witness — we pray that we also will be generous, that we will be concerned, that we will be humble as we give forth the gospel of our Lord, and that You would bless that outreach by Your Spirit. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
